choices: 25.0 percent to 30.0 percent
5.0 percent to 12.0 percent
19.5 percent to 23.5 percent
10.0 percent to 15.0 percent
questions.explanation: Permit-required atmosphere limits set an acceptable oxygen entry level of 19.5 percent to 23.5 percent; normal breathing air is 20.9 percent oxygen, and the space must never fall below 19.5 percent.
questions.citation: OSHA Permit-Required Confined Spaces standard, 29 CFR 1910.146, as reflected on the entry permit form; acceptable oxygen entry level is 19.5 percent to 23.5 percent.

questions.text: Who is responsible for preparing and signing the entry permit, verifying that all atmospheric tests are done, and ensuring conditions stay acceptable until the work is finished?
choices: The relief entrant
The equipment vendor
The attendant
The entry supervisor
questions.explanation: The entry supervisor prepares and signs the permit before entry, knows the hazards, verifies all atmospheric tests are done and all procedures and equipment are in place, and ensures acceptable conditions continue until the work is done.
questions.citation: OSHA Permit-Required Confined Spaces standard, 29 CFR 1910.146; the entry supervisor prepares and signs the permit, verifies atmospheric tests, and ensures acceptable conditions continue until work is done.

questions.text: How often must a confined-space rescue team practice a simulated rescue?
choices: At least once every 12 months
Only once, at initial certification
Every 5 years
Every 3 years, regardless of team turnover
questions.explanation: On-site rescue teams get the same training as entrants plus PPE and rescue-equipment use and first aid including CPR, and simulated rescues must be performed at least once every 12 months.
questions.citation: OSHA Permit-Required Confined Spaces standard, 29 CFR 1910.146; simulated rescues must be performed at least once every 12 months.

questions.text: Even when initial atmospheric testing shows a confined space is safe, what must still happen before and during entry?
choices: Nothing further is required once the initial test reads safe
The space must still be ventilated with forced-air, intrinsically safe equipment
The permit process may be skipped once the initial reading tests safe
Only a single entrant is allowed, with no attendant required, once the space tests safe
questions.explanation: Confined spaces must be ventilated with specially designed forced-air ventilators, even if instruments show the atmosphere is safe, and the blowers must be intrinsically safe so they cannot create a spark, since some of the gases involved are explosive.
questions.citation: OSHA Permit-Required Confined Spaces standard, 29 CFR 1910.146; confined spaces must be ventilated with forced-air ventilators, even if instruments show the atmosphere is safe, using intrinsically safe blowers.

questions.text: At what trench depth must a worker have access to a ladder positioned within 25 feet and extending at least 3 feet above the excavation wall?
choices: 15 feet or more
2 feet or more
4 feet or more
8 feet or more
questions.explanation: A ladder is required for each worker in a trench 4 feet or more deep, and the ladder must be within 25 feet of the worker and extend at least 3 feet above the excavation wall.
questions.citation: OSHA excavation standard, 29 CFR 1926.651(c)(2); a ladder is required for trenches 4 feet or more deep, positioned within 25 feet and extending at least 3 feet above the excavation.

questions.text: How far back from the trench edge must excavated spoil be placed, and on how many sides?
choices: At least 10 feet back, on both sides equally
At least 5 feet back, on both sides equally
At least 6 inches back, on one side only
At least 2 feet back, on one side only
questions.explanation: Spoil, the dirt removed from a trench, must be placed at least 2 feet back from the trench, on one side only.
questions.citation: OSHA excavation standard, 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P, as reflected in standard trench-safety training; spoil must be placed at least 2 feet back from the trench, on one side only.

questions.text: Which condition is documented as raising a trench's cave-in risk?
choices: A trench excavated in freezing or near-freezing conditions
A trench that runs parallel to an existing underground utility line
A trench less than 3 feet deep with sloped walls
A trench that has sat open, undisturbed, for less than one hour
questions.explanation: Conditions that raise cave-in risk include low cohesion, high moisture content, freezing conditions, and recent prior excavation at the same site, along with trench depth, soil weight, nearby equipment weight, and vibration.
questions.citation: OSHA excavation standard, 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P, as reflected in standard trench-safety training; freezing conditions, low cohesion, high moisture, and recent prior excavation raise cave-in risk.

questions.text: At approximately what concentration can hydrogen sulfide (H2S) cause death within a few minutes?
choices: 0.02 percent
0.2 percent
2.0 percent
0.002 percent
questions.explanation: Hydrogen sulfide causes death within a few minutes at a concentration of about 0.2 percent, since it paralyzes the respiratory center and, at high enough concentration, unconsciousness comes suddenly, followed by death without prompt rescue.
questions.citation: Oklahoma DEQ gas-hazard reference, cited in the Distribution and Collection Operator Study Guide; death occurs in a few minutes at 0.2 percent hydrogen sulfide.

questions.text: Why can an operator not rely on smell to detect a dangerous hydrogen sulfide (H2S) concentration?
choices: The rotten-egg smell gets stronger without limit as concentration rises, so smell is actually a reliable warning
H2S has no odor at any concentration, so smell was never a valid indicator
H2S paralyzes the sense of smell, so the odor becomes undetectable at the highest, most dangerous concentrations
H2S only smells dangerous when mixed with chlorine gas
questions.explanation: Hydrogen sulfide quickly numbs the sense of smell, so the rotten-egg odor fading does not mean the gas is gone; it can mean the opposite, which is why atmospheric monitoring, not the nose, must be trusted to judge H2S danger.
questions.citation: New England Interstate Water Pollution Control Commission, cited in Wastewater Collections for Small Water Systems digest; H2S quickly numbs the sense of smell, so a fading odor does not mean the gas is gone.

questions.text: What documented warning equipment should always be used by a crew working near traffic, alongside signs or a flagger?
choices: A single reflective cone, placed at the crew's discretion
A single strobe light mounted on the work vehicle
Cones and warning tape
High-visibility vests and flashing lights
questions.explanation: Traffic-control practice for collection-system work always calls for warnings including high-visibility vests and flashing lights, in addition to signs or a flagger and a physical barrier where practical.
questions.citation: Distribution and Collection Operator Study Guide, traffic-control practice for utility work near traffic; always use warnings including vests and flashing lights.

questions.text: How long should MIS (management information system) records for a collection-system maintenance program typically be kept?
choices: At least three years
At least three months
Only for the current fiscal quarter
No retention guideline exists
questions.explanation: MIS records should be kept at least three years, which supports the same 'if it's not recorded, it didn't happen' discipline applied to water-main break records.
questions.citation: US EPA, Guide for Evaluating CMOM Programs at Sanitary Sewer Collection Systems (EPA 305-B-05-002, January 2005); MIS records should be kept at least three years.

questions.text: A mainline sewer stoppage overflows out of a lateral cleanout rather than a mainline manhole. Which asset should the emergency work order credit?
choices: The lateral cleanout, since that is where the overflow physically surfaced
The main that actually failed, not just the lateral cleanout where the overflow surfaced
Overflow work orders are not tied to any specific asset
Whichever asset is closest to a paved street, regardless of where the failure occurred
questions.explanation: Track emergency work to the asset that actually failed, not just where the overflow surfaced, since many mainline stoppages overflow out of a lateral cleanout, so the correct main should get the credit.
questions.citation: CA Water Boards, SSORS Best Management Practices manual (2009); track emergency work to the asset that actually failed, not just where the overflow surfaced.

questions.text: When a utility changes how it records a particular field in its maintenance data, what should it document to keep long-term trends readable?
choices: Rewriting old records to match, with no note of the change date
Only the department name
The date the recording method changed
Only the name of the employee who requested the change
questions.explanation: When you change how you record something, document the change date so long-term trends stay readable, the same 'garbage in, garbage out' discipline that keeps years of maintenance data comparable.
questions.citation: CA Water Boards, SSORS Best Management Practices manual (2009); when you change how you record something, document the change date so long-term trends stay readable.

questions.text: What do routine security checks at an unstaffed lift station typically include?
choices: Only checking the station's electrical supply is functioning properly
Only checking for accumulated debris inside the station
Only checking that the control panel door is fully latched
Checking fencing, gates, locks, hatches, and panels for signs of tampering
questions.explanation: Standard collection-system security practice includes checking fencing, gates, locks, hatches, and control-panel doors for signs of forced entry or tampering as part of the routine site inspection, alongside the mechanical checks.
questions.citation: Standard collection-system security practice, consistent with widely used utility site-security preventive-maintenance guidance; routine checks include fencing, gates, locks, hatches, and control-panel doors for signs of tampering.

questions.text: A crew finds a manhole cover or lift-station hatch that has clearly been pried open, with no scheduled work to explain it. What is the correct immediate response?
choices: Report it as a possible security incident, per the tampering procedure
Assume it was scheduled work that was not logged, and take no further action
Quietly close it back up and say nothing, since it is probably nothing
Wait until the next annual inspection cycle to note it in passing
questions.explanation: Suspected tampering with a manhole cover or lift-station hatch should be treated as a possible security incident, reported per the utility's tampering-response procedure, and the access point should be inspected for damage or an unsafe condition before normal access resumes.
questions.citation: Standard collection-system security practice, consistent with widely used utility site-security preventive-maintenance guidance; suspected tampering should be reported and the site inspected before normal access resumes.
